item.title: Failings on defence leave Britain cruelly exposed
item.description: A desperate urge to save money has led the UK to ignore the dangerous new realities

item.title: How crowds become stupider
item.description: Prediction markets suffer when we all think the same way

item.title: Laying off cigarettes boosts Big Tobacco’s financial vitals
item.description: Despite the decline in smoker numbers, investors are rethinking the industry’s prospects

item.title: What has America ever done for us?
item.description: Jazz? The moon landings? Gerrymandering? On this 250th anniversary, it seems only right to think about this question

item.title: The June jobs report was a blip — we hope
item.description: Plus, tricks with trimmed mean inflation

item.title: Revolut and Monzo should heed Atom Bank’s not-so-tiny challenges
item.description: As these racy unicorns look to bulk up in lending, they will have to navigate some of the same complexities

item.title: Investing in an age of lawlessness
item.description: Copying the investments made by Trump and other political elites is one potential tactic but it carries risks

item.title: Adidas is outplaying Nike in the global sportswear competition
item.description: The momentum is finally with the German group under the leadership of Bjørn Gulden

item.title: All hail the King of the North! But which north?
item.description: Andy Burnham’s fantasy realm will fall apart if it’s allowed contact with messy geographical reality

item.title: ’Tis the season to discover the UK’s hidden garden gems
item.description: The National Garden Scheme offers the opportunity to peek into some little-known plantsman paradises

item.title: When food and fashion collide
item.description: Both get my juices flowing, but it’s something of an odd combination

item.title: The faltering engine of Germany’s auto industry
item.description: VW is a test of the country’s ability to adapt

item.title: The chip rally is tightening tech’s grip on Wall Street
item.description: AI spending frenzy has powered a semiconductor surge

item.title: On America’s 250th, remember your Adam Smith . . .
item.description: The ground is fertile for a renewal of the commitment to free markets and free people

item.title: Altman’s AI safety proposal: let us win, or everybody loses
item.description: A US-led world order would hand a big advantage to an American oligopoly that includes his own company

item.title: Eliminate ‘personal pricing’ and you risk harming consumers
item.description: Students, seniors and coupon savers could all lose out under plans to make prices fairer

item.title: There will be no more games like Minecraft without maths
item.description: Cuts to UK higher education courses are a danger to our tech industries

item.title: Andy Burnham and the politics of nostalgia
item.description: Starmer’s presumptive heir is locked in a battle over Britain’s past — and future — with Nigel Farage

item.title: A tour of the world through bite-sized fiction
item.description: A new Penguin anthology celebrates the universal appeal of the short story with 34 fine examples from dozens of countries

item.title: Fear, loathing and ice cream pricing
item.description: Japan’s food industry is a shopfront for the vulnerabilities of a weak currency

item.title: How AI could make markets worse
item.description: Heed the lessons of information economics

item.title: Woah-oh, we’re halfway there
item.description: A strong first half (until the last bit)

item.title: How do we save the wilting new homes market?
item.description: Like it or not, the government will have to help developers before houses and rents become even more unaffordable

item.title: The economics of women’s weight
item.description: A new study suggests weight-loss drugs help women find work — and prompt men to leave their partners

item.title: Can Kevin Warsh tame the Fed’s $7tn balance sheet?
item.description: Pragmatism, not ideology, should be the guide
